title: Standardize and harden GDPR internal endpoint authentication and auditing
description: Standardize authentication and auditing for all GDPR internal endpoints across all backend services. Replace inconsistent header checks with a unified authentication mechanism (e.g., internal service token or mTLS). Require a confirmation nonce for DELETE operations, and log all GDPR requests to the audit schema with service identity and timestamp. Consider implementing two-party authorization for GDPR delete operations.
subtasks: Implement a shared authentication mechanism (e.g., internal service token or mTLS) for all /internal/gdpr/export/:id and /internal/gdpr/delete/:id endpoints across all services.
Remove ad-hoc header checks (e.g., X-Internal-Request, X-Internal-Key) and replace with the unified mechanism.
Require a confirmation nonce for DELETE operations to prevent accidental or replayed deletions.
Log every GDPR export and delete request to the audit schema with requesting service identity and timestamp.
Document and, if feasible, implement a two-party authorization flow for GDPR delete (e.g., Morgan requests, human confirms via Signal).
Run integration tests to verify that only authenticated internal requests can access GDPR endpoints and that all actions are logged.
Run OWASP ZAP scan against all /internal/gdpr/* endpoints to verify no unauthorized access is possible.
acceptance_criteria: All /internal/gdpr/export/:id and /internal/gdpr/delete/:id endpoints require the new authentication mechanism.
DELETE operations require a confirmation nonce and cannot be replayed.
All GDPR requests are logged to the audit schema with service identity and timestamp.
Two-party authorization is required for GDPR delete (if implemented).
OWASP ZAP scan shows no unauthorized access to GDPR endpoints.
Integration tests confirm only authenticated internal requests succeed.

title: Implement asymmetric JWT signing, key rotation, and claim validation
description: Replace the shared symmetric JWT_SECRET with an asymmetric signing approach (RS256/ES256) where an identity service holds the private key and all services validate using a JWKS endpoint. If HS256 must be used for v1, add issuer (iss) and audience (aud) claim validation in each service's JWT middleware. Add JWT_SECRET to the secret rotation runbook with a dual-key rollover procedure.
subtasks: Implement RS256/ES256 JWT signing in the identity/auth service, exposing a JWKS endpoint for public key distribution.
Update all backend services to validate JWTs using the JWKS endpoint and enforce RS256/ES256 algorithms.
If asymmetric signing is not feasible for v1, add iss and aud claim validation to all JWT middleware.
Document and implement a JWT key rotation procedure, supporting dual-key rollover.
Add JWT_SECRET to the secret rotation runbook and ensure it is included in the rotation CronJob.
Write integration tests to verify that only tokens with correct iss/aud and valid signatures are accepted.
Run security tests to ensure that a compromised service cannot mint valid tokens for other services.
acceptance_criteria: All services validate JWTs using RS256/ES256 and the JWKS endpoint, or enforce iss/aud validation if HS256 is used.
JWT key rotation procedure is documented and tested, supporting dual-key rollover.
JWT_SECRET is included in the secret rotation CronJob and runbook.
Integration tests confirm only valid tokens are accepted.
Security tests confirm that a compromised service cannot mint tokens for others.

title: Pin all container images and base images to digests or version tags and enforce supply chain scanning
description: Update all container image references to use pinned version tags or digest hashes instead of :latest. Ensure all base images in Dockerfiles are pinned to specific versions or digests. Configure CI to run Trivy or Grype container image scanning before pushing to the registry. Use a private container registry and pull base images through a mirroring/scanning proxy.
subtasks: Update all Kubernetes manifests and Dockerfiles to use pinned image tags or digests (e.g., image@sha256:...).
Pin all base images in Dockerfiles to specific versions or digests.
Configure CI pipeline to run Trivy or Grype scans on all built images before pushing to the registry.
Set up or document use of a private container registry and mirroring/scanning proxy for base images.
Verify that no :latest tags remain in any deployment or Dockerfile.
Run a full CI pipeline to ensure image scanning passes for all services.
acceptance_criteria: All container images and base images are pinned to version tags or digests.
CI pipeline runs Trivy or Grype scans and fails on critical/high findings.
No :latest tags are present in any deployment or Dockerfile.
All images are pulled from a private registry or through a scanning proxy.
CI pipeline passes with all images scanned and no critical/high vulnerabilities.

title: Harden ConfigMap and Secret usage for connection strings and sensitive endpoints
description: Move all sensitive endpoint URLs and connection strings (including Valkey, PostgreSQL, R2, Signal-CLI) from ConfigMaps to Kubernetes Secrets. Apply automountServiceAccountToken: false from initial infrastructure setup, not just in production. Use projected volumes to mount only the specific secrets each service needs.
subtasks: Refactor infrastructure manifests to store all sensitive connection strings in Kubernetes Secrets instead of ConfigMaps.
Update service Deployments to mount only the required secrets via projected volumes, not envFrom of all keys.
Set automountServiceAccountToken: false on all service pods from the initial deployment phase.
Audit all existing ConfigMaps for sensitive data and migrate as needed.
Test that only the intended pods can access their required secrets.
Run a Kubernetes RBAC and secret access audit to verify least privilege.
acceptance_criteria: All sensitive connection strings and endpoint URLs are stored in Secrets, not ConfigMaps.
Service pods mount only the secrets they require, not all keys.
automountServiceAccountToken: false is set on all service pods from initial deployment.
No sensitive data remains in any ConfigMap.
RBAC and secret access audit confirms least privilege.

title: Enable Valkey authentication and enforce secret storage for all environments
description: Enable authentication for Valkey (Redis) in all environments, including development. Store the Valkey password in a Kubernetes Secret and configure Valkey with requirepass. Update VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL to include the credential reference. Ensure Valkey password is rotated alongside other secrets.
subtasks: Configure Valkey with requirepass in all environments.
Store the Valkey password in a Kubernetes Secret.
Update all service configurations to use VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL with the password from the secret.
Add Valkey password to the secret rotation CronJob and runbook.
Test that unauthenticated access to Valkey is rejected in all environments.
Run a security scan to verify no services can connect to Valkey without authentication.
acceptance_criteria: Valkey requires authentication in all environments.
Valkey password is stored in a Kubernetes Secret and not in any ConfigMap.
All services use the updated VALKEY_SIGMA1_URL with authentication.
Valkey password is included in the secret rotation process.
Unauthenticated access to Valkey is rejected in all environments.

title: Enforce dependency pinning and lockfile verification for Go and Node.js services
description: Commit and verify go.sum for Go services and package-lock.json or bun.lockb for Node.js services. Enforce lockfile verification in CI. Move dependency auditing (cargo-audit, nancy, npm audit) to the PR-level CI pipeline. Enable Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs.
subtasks: Ensure go.sum is committed for all Go services and verify it in CI using go mod verify.
Ensure package-lock.json or bun.lockb is committed for all Node.js services and enforce --frozen-lockfile in CI.
Move dependency auditing tools (cargo-audit, nancy, npm audit) to run on every PR in the quality.yml workflow.
Enable Dependabot or Renovate for all repositories to automate dependency update PRs.
Test that CI fails if lockfiles are missing or out of sync.
Run a test PR to verify dependency audit tools block merges on critical/high findings.
acceptance_criteria: go.sum and package-lock.json/bun.lockb are committed and verified in CI.
Dependency audit tools run on every PR and block merges on critical/high findings.
Dependabot or Renovate is enabled and creates update PRs.
CI fails if lockfiles are missing or not up to date.

title: Implement column-level encryption for PII and document PII data map
description: Encrypt sensitive PII columns (email, phone, worker_name, etc.) using pgcrypto at the application level before storage. Ensure the CNPG cluster has encryption at rest enabled at the storage level. Document a PII data map showing which schemas contain what PII types.
subtasks: Identify all PII columns across schemas and update application logic to encrypt them using pgcrypto before storage.
Store encryption keys separately from database credentials and document key management procedures.
Verify that the CNPG cluster's storageClass has encryption at rest enabled.
Create and maintain a PII data map documenting which schemas and columns contain PII.
Test that encrypted columns cannot be read in plaintext from the database.
Run a database audit to verify all PII columns are encrypted.
acceptance_criteria: All sensitive PII columns are encrypted at the application level using pgcrypto.
Encryption keys are managed separately from database credentials.
CNPG cluster storage is encrypted at rest.
A PII data map is available and up to date.
Database audit confirms all PII columns are encrypted.

title: Harden Signal-CLI pod and PVC with encryption and least privilege
description: Use an encrypted StorageClass for the signal-cli-data PVC. Add securityContext to the Signal-CLI pod to run as non-root and with a read-only root filesystem. Restrict PVC access to only the Signal-CLI pod via a dedicated ServiceAccount and RBAC.
subtasks: Update the signal-cli-data PVC to use an encrypted StorageClass.
Set securityContext in the Signal-CLI pod: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true.
Mount a writable emptyDir for temp files as needed.
Create a dedicated ServiceAccount and RBAC Role/RoleBinding to restrict PVC access to only the Signal-CLI pod.
Test that the pod runs as non-root and cannot write to the root filesystem.
Verify that only the Signal-CLI pod can access the PVC.
acceptance_criteria: signal-cli-data PVC uses an encrypted StorageClass.
Signal-CLI pod runs as non-root with read-only root filesystem.
Only the Signal-CLI pod can access the PVC.
Pod securityContext is enforced and verified.

title: Enforce Pod Security Standards at the namespace level
description: Apply Kubernetes Pod Security Standards at the namespace level using labels (pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted) for all namespaces except openclaw (which may need baseline). Enforce runAsNonRoot, drop ALL capabilities, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and disallow hostNetwork/hostPID.
subtasks: Label all namespaces (except openclaw) with pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted.
Apply PodSecurityPolicy, Kyverno, or OPA policies to enforce runAsNonRoot, drop ALL capabilities, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and disallow hostNetwork/hostPID.
Document any exceptions for openclaw namespace if baseline is required.
Test that pods violating these standards are rejected.
Run a security audit to verify all namespaces enforce the required standards.
acceptance_criteria: All namespaces (except openclaw) enforce restricted Pod Security Standards.
Pods violating standards are rejected.
Security audit confirms enforcement.

title: Implement centralized audit logging for security-sensitive operations
description: Create a dedicated audit.security_events table or structured logging pipeline to Loki for failed authentication attempts, admin mutations, GDPR operations, external API credential usage, and rate limit triggers. Include timestamp, service, event_type, actor_id, resource_id, source_ip, and outcome. Add a Grafana dashboard panel for security events and alert on anomalies.
subtasks: Design and implement a centralized audit log table (audit.security_events) or configure structured logging to Loki.
Update all services to log security-sensitive events (auth failures, admin actions, GDPR ops, external API usage, rate limit triggers) to the audit log.
Include all required fields: timestamp, service, event_type, actor_id, resource_id, source_ip, outcome.
Configure Grafana dashboard panel to visualize security events.
Set up alerting for anomalies (e.g., >10 auth failures per minute from same IP).
Test that all relevant events are logged and visible in Grafana.
acceptance_criteria: All security-sensitive operations are logged centrally.
Audit log includes all required fields.
Grafana dashboard panel displays security events.
Alerts fire on defined anomalies.
Test events are visible in the audit log and dashboard.

title: Harden input validation and output encoding for user-facing text fields
description: Ensure all services use parameterized queries only (no string concatenation for SQL). Review dynamic QueryBuilder usage to ensure bind parameters are used. Add output encoding/sanitization for any user-supplied text rendered in the Next.js frontend. Add a shared input validation library or middleware to strip or reject control characters and excessively long inputs.
subtasks: Review all dynamic SQL query construction to ensure only parameterized queries are used.
Audit and refactor any string interpolation in SQL queries, especially in dynamic QueryBuilder usage.
Implement output encoding/sanitization for user-supplied text in the Next.js frontend, prohibiting dangerouslySetInnerHTML.
Develop or integrate a shared input validation middleware/library for all services.
Test for SQL injection and XSS vulnerabilities using automated tools (e.g., sqlmap, ZAP).
Write unit and integration tests for input validation and output encoding.
acceptance_criteria: All SQL queries are parameterized; no string concatenation is used.
All user-supplied text rendered in the frontend is properly encoded/sanitized.
Input validation middleware is in place and effective.
Automated security tests show no SQL injection or XSS vulnerabilities.

title: Harden Stripe webhook endpoint with official signature validation, replay protection, and rate limiting
description: Use the official stripe-rust crate's webhook signature verification. Add explicit replay protection by rejecting events with old timestamps. Apply a separate rate limit to the webhook endpoint and store processed Stripe event IDs in a deduplication set (Valkey with TTL) to prevent duplicate processing.
subtasks: Refactor Stripe webhook handler to use the official stripe-rust crate's verify_webhook function for signature validation.
Implement replay protection by rejecting events where the timestamp header is older than 5 minutes.
Apply a dedicated rate limiter to the webhook endpoint (e.g., 50 req/s).
Store processed Stripe event IDs in Valkey with a TTL to prevent duplicate processing.
Test webhook endpoint with valid, invalid, replayed, and duplicate events.
Run OWASP ZAP scan against the webhook endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: Stripe webhook uses official signature validation.
Replay protection and rate limiting are enforced.
Duplicate events are not processed.
Security tests confirm no bypass of validation or rate limits.

title: Harden file upload endpoint with comprehensive validation and malware scanning
description: Add file size limits, file type validation (magic bytes), virus/malware scanning, filename sanitization, and limits on number of files per request to the file upload endpoint. Set Content-Disposition: attachment on R2 objects to prevent inline rendering of potentially malicious content.
subtasks: Enforce file size limits (e.g., 20MB per file, 100MB per request) at both the body parser and upload handler levels.
Validate file magic bytes to ensure only allowed types (JPEG, PNG) are accepted.
Sanitize filenames to strip path traversal and unsafe characters.
Integrate ClamAV scanning as a sidecar or pre-processing step before R2 upload.
Set Content-Disposition: attachment on all uploaded R2 objects.
Test file upload endpoint with oversized, invalid type, and malicious files.
Run automated security scans (e.g., ZAP, ClamAV) against the upload endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: File size, type, and count limits are enforced.
Only valid JPEG/PNG files are accepted.
Filenames are sanitized.
All files are scanned for malware before upload.
Content-Disposition: attachment is set on all R2 objects.
Security tests confirm no malicious files can be uploaded or served inline.

title: Document and enforce third-party data processing agreements and consent for PII sent to external APIs
description: Document all third-party APIs that receive PII or customer data. Ensure data processing agreements (DPAs) are in place with OpenAI, OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, and Google. Use OpenAI's data usage policies to opt out of training. Add a consent mechanism for customers whose data will be sent to third-party vetting services. Evaluate self-hosted LLM alternatives for photo scoring.
subtasks: Create and maintain documentation listing all third-party APIs receiving PII or customer data.
Ensure DPAs are signed and on file for OpenAI, OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, and Google.
Configure OpenAI API calls to opt out of data training where possible.
Implement a customer consent mechanism for data sent to third-party vetting services.
Evaluate and document the feasibility of self-hosted LLM alternatives for photo scoring.
Test that all API calls to OpenAI and other third parties include appropriate data usage parameters.
acceptance_criteria: Documentation of all third-party PII data flows is complete and up to date.
DPAs are in place for all relevant third parties.
OpenAI API calls opt out of data training.
Customer consent is required and recorded before sending PII to third-party vetting.
Feasibility of self-hosted LLM alternatives is documented.
